title: Image value not used for health-startup-cmd
body:
### Issue Description

For health-cmd we have:

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/ed132b7a720e6562d54646081f80f685338f15c5/docs/source/markdown/options/health-cmd.md#L14

For health-startup-cmd there's no documentation that the configuration from the image would be used and this matches how at least 5.4.2 seems to behave https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/ed132b7a720e6562d54646081f80f685338f15c5/docs/source/markdown/options/health-startup-cmd.md



### Steps to reproduce the issue

Given an image built with buildkit:

```
$ docker inspect -f '{{json .Config.Healthcheck}}' $IMAGE
{"Test":["CMD-SHELL","pg_isready -U postgres"],"Interval":3600000000000,"StartPeriod":60000000000,"StartInterval":1000000000}
```


### Describe the results you received

With docker pg_isready is run every second:

```json
            "Health": {
                "Status": "healthy",
                "FailingStreak": 0,
                "Log": [
                    {
                        "Start": "2025-12-09T12:03:03.836191549Z",
                        "End": "2025-12-09T12:03:04.227778507Z",
                        "ExitCode": 2,
                        "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - no response\n"
                    },
                    {
                        "Start": "2025-12-09T12:03:05.229269716Z",
                        "End": "2025-12-09T12:03:05.4471183Z",
                        "ExitCode": 2,
                        "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - no response\n"
                    },
                    {
                        "Start": "2025-12-09T12:03:06.448626008Z",
                        "End": "2025-12-09T12:03:06.691341842Z",
                        "ExitCode": 0,
                        "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
                    }
                ]
            }
```

With podman it's only run once and then scheduled 

```
2025-12-09 11:14:41.340943129 +0000 UTC container health_status feeb1bb73a5b00ea57ace734c4d4def9a4cb76631f1a2b2877d0655fc47a0005 (image=IMAGE, name=postgres, health_status=starting, health_failing_streak=0, health_log=, PODMAN_SYSTEMD_UNIT=postgres.service)
```

### Describe the results you expected

Podman behavior should match Docker.

### podman info output


### Podman in a container

### Privileged Or Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

If later release has fixed the behaviour, at minimum the documentation in main need updating.

comments.body: This sounds like a documentation issue. Images do not have a concept of startup healthchecks - they're exclusively supported by our CLI and Kubernetes YAML. I would not expect us to be configuring them based on image values.

> This sounds like a documentation issue. Images do not have a concept of startup healthchecks - they're exclusively supported by our CLI and Kubernetes YAML. I would not expect us to be configuring them based on image values.

StartPeriod and StartInterval can come from the image:

```
podman inspect -f '{{json .HealthCheck}}' xxxxxxxx
{"Test":["CMD-SHELL","pg_isready -U postgres"],"StartPeriod":60000000000,"StartInterval":1000000000,"Interval":3600000000000}
```

I think docker takes the more logical approach that when StartInterval is configured, Test value is used as the startup healthcheck command.

I think you're confusing the startup healthcheck and the normal healthcheck's start period.

The startup healthcheck is an entirely different healthcheck that runs prior to the main healthcheck. Only after it succeeds does the main healthcheck commence. Docker does not include startup healthcheck support, and there is no support for it in the image spec.

Normal healthchecks also have startup features. They can delay reporting results (start period). This is not a startup healthcheck; a startup healthcheck is an entirely different healthcheck command, and is configured separately.

Docker appears to have added a new `--health-startup-interval` option to have the normal healthcheck run at a different interval during container start, which we do not currently support in Podman; I'm going to tag this as an RFE to implement support. We'll likely also need support in Buildah, as I don't believe we can create images with this new config bit.

Hello,
Do you have any strict target date for this feature? If no, I would like to implement this feature
No dates at present, feel free to grab this one!

Hello @mheon 
I'm trying to understand what we are going to implement exactly.
There are two kind of health checks in podman at this moment: startup health check (retrieved with the command defined in `--health-startup-cmd`) and ordinary normal health check (retrieved with the command defined in `--health-cmd`). 
Each of them have their own run intervals, and they all work as excepted.
So, what is the issue here?
As far as I understood we are going to use a command defined in `--health-startup-cmd` for the startup and normal health checks, with no the necessity toaditionaly add the same command for `--health-cmd`.
Am I right?

> Hello [@mheon](https://github.com/mheon) I'm trying to understand what we are going to implement exactly. There are two kind of health checks in podman at this moment: startup health check (retrieved with the command defined in `--health-startup-cmd`) and ordinary normal health check (retrieved with the command defined in `--health-cmd`). Each of them have their own run intervals, and they all work as excepted. So, what is the issue here? 

These commands/settings can also be baked to the image. New code should at least pick up values coming from Docker build tools and use them appropriately.

> > Hello [@mheon](https://github.com/mheon) I'm trying to understand what we are going to implement exactly. There are two kind of health checks in podman at this moment: startup health check (retrieved with the command defined in `--health-startup-cmd`) and ordinary normal health check (retrieved with the command defined in `--health-cmd`). Each of them have their own run intervals, and they all work as excepted. So, what is the issue here?
> 
> These commands/settings can also be baked to the image. New code should at least pick up values coming from Docker build tools and use them appropriately.

Hello,

Yes it is possible. However,  `Dockerfile` specification doesn't differ commands for startup 
health check and for normal health check. Moreover, there is must be only one `HEALTHCHECK` instruction. Yes, all those instructions are taken into account by `podman` and work fine:

The test `Dockerfile`
```
FROM postgres

HEALTHCHECK --interval=20s \
            --start-period=60s \ 
            --start-interval=10s \
            CMD pg_isready -U postgres

ENTRYPOINT ["docker-entrypoint.sh"]
CMD ["postgres"]
```

`podman` output:
```
$ podman inspect pgsql -f '{{json .State.Health}}' | jq
{
  "Status": "healthy",
  "FailingStreak": 0,
  "Log": [
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T17:42:02.386648824+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T17:42:02.51598867+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T17:42:23.282237495+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T17:42:23.384139854+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T17:42:44.287477158+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T17:42:44.398672892+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T17:43:05.362383699+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T17:43:05.468689951+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T17:43:26.344621725+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T17:43:26.481222955+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    }
  ]
}
```

> 
> `podman` output:
> 

Your output only has passing healthchecks. Try the values (use shorter intervals) so that you get failing output to the logs.

> > `podman` output:
> 
> Your output only has passing healthchecks. Try the values (use shorter intervals) so that you get failing output to the logs.

Yes, it was the recent output.
The initial output was the following, and there was the failed probe:

```
$ podman inspect pgsql -f '{{json .State.Health}}' | jq
{
  "Status": "healthy",
  "FailingStreak": 0,
  "Log": [
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T16:15:56.65214188+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T16:15:56.806331949+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 1,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - no response\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T16:16:17.3442776+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T16:16:17.544158767+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T16:16:38.401713532+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T16:16:38.592064809+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    }
  ]
}
$
```

I'm pretty sure we don't support this right now? We have startup healthchecks, and a mechanism to transition from the startup healthcheck to a normal healthcheck, but I'm fairly confident we don't have a way of altering the interval of an already-running healthcheck? We also don't have a way of actually setting startup period (Docker provides a `--health-start-interval` option that we don't). Implementing this would allow you to check whether the functionality works (set a start interval of 1s and a normal interval of 60s, and see how often the healthcheck is firing, for example?)

> We also don't have a way of actually setting startup period (Docker provides a `--health-start-interval` option that we don't).

We have `--health-startup-interval` (Set an interval for the startup healthcheck.).
It provides the same capability as Docker's `--health-start-interval` does: set the interval between health check **during container starting stage**. Isn't it?

> 
> Yes, it was the recent output. The initial output was the following, and there was the failed probe:
> 

The start times are as follows for seconds:
 * 56
 * 17
 * 38

These are all 20s apart. There's no 10s interval in the output that your settings would call for.

> > Yes, it was the recent output. The initial output was the following, and there was the failed probe:
> 
> The start times are as follows for seconds:
> 
>     * 56
> 
>     * 17
> 
>     * 38
> 
> 
> These are all 20s apart. There's no 10s interval in the output that your settings would call for. I set 

Yes, the same for `Docker`:

```
$ docker inspect pgsql -f '{{json .State.Health}}' | jq
{
  "Status": "healthy",
  "FailingStreak": 0,
  "Log": [
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T19:27:01.016240764+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T19:27:01.160232347+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T19:27:21.161201546+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T19:27:21.285008617+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T19:27:41.285739055+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T19:27:41.450810502+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 0,
      "Output": "/var/run/postgresql:5432 - accepting connections\n"
    }
  ]
}
```

> 
> Yes, the same for `Docker`:
> 

You again posted log output with only passing checks.

Here is what I've just observed:

1) when `--health-interval`, `--health-cmd`, `--health-start-period`, `--health-startup-interval` and `--health-startup-cmd` are set in `podman run`, then everything works fine: startup command runs within defined interval.
2) when `--health-startup-cmd`  in not set, then only `--health-cmd` is used and it runs within the time which is set in `--health-interval`, consequently  `--health-startup-interval` is ignored.


> > Yes, the same for `Docker`:
> 
> You again posted log output with only passing checks.

That's because startup health check succeeded, and it transited to normal health check.
In case of failure of startup health check then `Docker` tries to execute the health check within start period interval:
```
$ docker run --name pgsql --health-start-interval=10s --health-start-period=60s --health-cmd 'stat /tmp/test' --health-interval=20s -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=qwerty -d postgres
$
$
$ docker inspect pgsql -f '{{json .State.Health}}' | jq
{
  "Status": "starting",
  "FailingStreak": 0,
  "Log": [
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T20:13:47.719319565+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T20:13:47.850884135+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 1,
      "Output": "stat: cannot statx '/tmp/test': No such file or directory\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T20:13:57.851857406+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T20:13:57.943759665+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 1,
      "Output": "stat: cannot statx '/tmp/test': No such file or directory\n"
    },
    {
      "Start": "2025-12-24T20:14:07.945098252+02:00",
      "End": "2025-12-24T20:14:08.00530542+02:00",
      "ExitCode": 1,
      "Output": "stat: cannot statx '/tmp/test': No such file or directory\n"
    }
  ]
}
```

Conclusion: `podman` ignores health check command when options `--health-start-period`, `--health-startup-interval` are set, but  `--health-startup-cmd` is not present.
Good that you repeated but you probably would have saved time by noticing the your conclusions are already documented in the issue description.

> Good that you repeated but you probably would have saved time by noticing the your conclusions are already documented in the issue description.

Yeah, however I needed to get familiar with all comments in this issue.
